rulings.Case Title: GARY SHORT et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B*P) AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Sequoia Ventures Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment (Continued From Nov-20-2007)
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, NOVEMBER 21, 2007, LINE 11. DEFENDANT SEQUOIA VENTURE, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT: Motion is granted. Defendant shifted the burden of proof by addressing the only apparent claim disclosed by plaintiff's discovery responses. Once plaintiff articulated a new theory of liability in its opposition, defendant had the right to address the inadequacies of plaintiff's evidentiary support for its theory. The Court does not find persuasive plaintiff's argument that the Antioch plant is a product. Even if that were true, defendant is entitled to summary judgment for plaintiff has failed to offer expert testimony from an expert capable of testifying as to the inadequacies of defendant's design and construction of this plant. Such testimony is required under Miller v. Los Angeles Cty. Flood Control Dist. (1973) 8 Cal.3d 689 regardless of what legal theory plaintiff pursues. In the words of Justice Sullivan, "[W]e are satisfied that it was not for nonexpert minds to determine whether Noble Manors failed to exercise due care in the construction of the home. Building homes is a complicated activity. The average layman has neither training nor experience in the construction industry and ordinarily cannot determine whether a particular building has been built with the requisite skill and in accordance with the standards prescribed by law or prevailing in the industry." (At 702-703.) If building a home is complex, a power plant is far more complex. The decision in Hyman v. Gordon (1973) 35 Cal.App.3d 769, 775, upon which plaintiff relies, specifically notes the importance of expert testimony under Short's theory of liability. The Court concludes the fact that a CCP § 411.35 certificate was not filed by plaintiff when this action was filed is not determinative on this record. No motion for judgment on the pleadings has been filed in connection with the motion for summary judgment. The decision in Dow v. Holly Manufacturing Company (1958

rulings.Case Title: BARBARA JOHNSON VS. NORTHWEST AIRLINES, INC. et al
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, NOVEMBER 21, 2007, LINE 8. DEFENDANT NORTHWEST AIRLINES, INC. MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE IS DENIED. THE DECISIONS IN PACIFIC AIR ARE NOT CONTROLLING BECAUSE DEFENDANT HAS NOT DESIGNATED A PRINCIPAL PLACE OF BUSINESS IN CALIFORNIA. AS A CONSEQUENECE, THE DECISION IN BEUTKE V AMERICAN SECURITIES (1955) 132 CAL APP 2D 354, 361 RECOGNIZES PLAINTIFF'S CHOICE OF VENUE. THE REASONING OF BEUTKE IS APPLICABLE TO BOTH CONTRACT AND TORT CLAIMS. =(302/PJM/AY)
